✦ Book ✦

The Name of the Rose by Umberto Eco

12 April 2024 Umberto Eco Historical Mystery

Umberto Eco's first novel — published in Italian in 1980, translated into English in 1983 — is one of those rare works that operates successfully on several levels at once, and rewards the reader richly on every one of them.

On the surface it is a detective story. The year is 1327, and the Benedictine monk William of Baskerville (yes, the name is intentional) arrives at a remote Italian abbey to attend a theological disputation. Before the meetings can begin, a monk is found dead. Then another. The deaths multiply, and William — who has the deductive temperament of a certain fictional detective from Baker Street, several centuries before that detective was born — begins to investigate.

The Library

At the heart of the novel, literally and figuratively, is the abbey's vast labyrinthine library: one of the great repositories of knowledge in the medieval world, accessible only to those permitted by the librarian, its deeper sections never visited by most of the monks who have lived there for decades.

The library is a magnificent creation — part puzzle, part symbol — and Eco, who was a semiotician and medieval scholar before he was a novelist, brings enormous erudition to its depiction. The history of books, manuscripts, and the transmission of knowledge through the Middle Ages is rendered here not as background decoration but as the beating heart of the plot.

What the Novel Is Really About

The mystery, when it is solved, turns on the question of whether certain knowledge should exist at all — whether a book can be dangerous not because of what it teaches but simply because it exists, and might cause people to ask questions.

Eco's answer, implicit but unmistakable, is that the suppression of knowledge is always a greater sin than the knowledge itself.

The final image — the library burning, centuries of irreplaceable manuscripts feeding the flames — has stayed with me longer than almost any other ending I have read.

Rating: ✦ ✦ ✦ ✦ ✦
